heroOverview: Onycholysis is the painless separation of the nail plate from its underlying nail bed, beginning at the free edge and spreading proximally to create a whitish, opaque pocket beneath the nail. It affects an estimated 5-10% of the general population and is one of the most common nail disorders seen in dermatology practice. The condition itself is a sign rather than a standalone disease: trauma, fungal infection, psoriasis, thyroid dysfunction, and certain medications each account for a distinct subset of cases. Because the lifted nail creates a warm, moist cavity that bacteria and yeast readily colonise, secondary infection frequently complicates and prolongs the course. Most cases resolve once the underlying cause is identified and removed, but full regrowth of a toenail can take 12-18 months.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated to affect 5-10% of the general population; among the most frequent nail complaints in dermatology clinics worldwide
keyStats.demographics: More common in women than men, largely because acrylic nails, nail cosmetics, and manicure trauma are leading causes
keyStats.avgAge: Peaks in adults aged 30-60, though any age group can be affected
keyStats.globalCases: Exact global figures are unavailable; onycholysis accounts for a substantial proportion of nail disorder consultations across all regions

definition: Onycholysis (ICD-10: L60.1) is the separation of the nail plate from the nail bed, characterised by a distal or lateral detachment that progresses proximally and produces a pale, whitish, translucent to opaque appearance where the normal pink vascular nail bed is no longer visible beneath the plate. The separated space is prone to filling with air, keratin debris, moisture, and secondary organisms including Candida albicans and Pseudomonas aeruginosa; Pseudomonas pigment turns the nail green, giving a distinctive green-nail syndrome presentation. Onycholysis falls under dermatology and, when associated with systemic disease, may also involve endocrinology or rheumatology. It most often affects the fingernails, particularly the third and fourth digits, and the hallux toenail. The condition is classified as primary (idiopathic) or secondary to an identifiable cause. Secondary onycholysis is far more prevalent and its management hinges on treating the precipitating condition. Without addressing the cause, mechanical trimming alone only delays resolution.

typesClassification.description: The most common subtype in women; caused by repeated pressure, overlong nails, aggressive manicure, or acrylics lifting the free edge. Typically affects the third and fourth fingernails.
Dermatophyte invasion of the nail bed causes subungual hyperkeratosis and plate lifting; usually begins distally and is frequently bilateral in toenails.
Psoriasis causes 'oil-drop' translucent patches and pitting alongside onycholysis; nail psoriasis is present in up to 50% of psoriasis patients and in 80-90% of those with psoriatic arthritis.
Certain drugs (tetracyclines, psoralens, fluoroquinolones) sensitise the nail bed to UV light, producing acute separation after sun exposure; typically symmetric and fingernail-predominant.
Thyroid disease — both hyperthyroidism and, less often, hypothyroidism — is a recognised systemic cause; Plummer's nails (hyperthyroid onycholysis) classically affects the ring finger.

primarySymptoms: Painless lifting of the nail plate away from the nail bed, starting at the free edge and sometimes spreading to involve most of the nail.
Whitish, yellowish, or opaque discolouration of the separated portion, contrasting with the normal pink of the attached nail.
Accumulation of debris, keratin, or dirt beneath the lifted nail, giving the appearance of a thickened or dirty nail.
Green discolouration of the nail (green-nail syndrome) when Pseudomonas aeruginosa colonises the moist subungual space.
A notched or irregular free edge where the separation creates a visible gap between nail and skin.
Occasional mild tenderness or soreness when the separated nail is pressed or catches on clothing, despite the separation itself being painless.
Thickening of the nail plate in long-standing cases as the nail bed keratin accumulates beneath the detached plate.
Pitting or oil-drop discolouration of adjacent nails when onycholysis is part of nail psoriasis.
Brittle or crumbling edges at the point of separation, particularly in fungal-related cases.
Widening of the white crescent (lunula) appearance due to the spreading opaque zone toward the base of the nail.
earlyWarningSigns: A small, pale crescent at the free edge of the nail that appears without trauma or obvious reason.
The nail edge beginning to catch on fabric or lift slightly when touched.
A faint yellowish tinge beneath one or two fingernails not explained by nail polish.
Subtle softening or slight looseness of a fingernail noticed during routine hand washing.
emergencySigns: Rapid extension of green discolouration with swelling and warmth around the nail fold, suggesting acute Pseudomonas paronychia.
Fever, red streaking, or swelling tracking up the finger or hand, indicating secondary bacterial infection spreading beyond the nail.
Complete avulsion of the nail plate with exposed, bleeding nail bed.

causes.description: Repetitive pressure from tight footwear, overlong nails catching on surfaces, aggressive manicuring, and acrylic or gel nail products mechanically shear the nail plate from the bed. This is the single most common cause in women aged 20-50 and accounts for the majority of fingernail cases seen in primary care.

Dermatophytes — most often Trichophyton rubrum — invade the nail bed from the distal-lateral edge and produce subungual hyperkeratosis that lifts the overlying plate. Fungal onycholysis accounts for roughly 50% of all nail disorders and predominantly affects toenails in adults over 50.

Psoriatic plaques forming in the nail bed disrupt the tight attachment between bed epithelium and the nail plate underside. Up to 50% of people with cutaneous psoriasis develop nail changes, and onycholysis is one of the earliest and most prevalent nail psoriasis signs.

Both hyperthyroidism and hypothyroidism alter nail growth and adhesion. Hyperthyroidism-related Plummer's nails are characterised by soft, fast-growing nails that separate from the distal end, classically on the ring fingers. The mechanism involves increased nail matrix activity outpacing bed attachment.

Tetracyclines (especially doxycycline), psoralens, chlorpromazine, and fluoroquinolones accumulate in the nail unit and generate reactive oxygen species on UV exposure, disrupting the nail bed epithelium and causing acute separation within weeks of sun exposure.

Prolonged exposure to water, detergents, solvents, acrylic nail adhesives, and nail hardeners damages the nail bed epithelium and weakens its attachment to the plate, particularly common in healthcare workers, hairdressers, and those with frequent wet-work exposure.

While not a primary cause, once the subungual space is created by any mechanism, Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Candida albicans readily colonise the warm moist gap. Their presence perpetuates and worsens the separation, producing the green nail or a chronic onycholysis that fails to resolve until the organisms are eradicated.

riskFactors.factor: Use of acrylic, gel, or sculptured nails
riskFactors.description: Artificial nail products physically bond to the natural plate and can mechanically separate it during application, maintenance, or removal. Studies of nail salon workers and clients show rates of onycholysis two to three times higher than in the general population.

Healthcare workers, cleaners, cooks, and hairdressers with hands submerged in water or chemicals for extended daily periods suffer maceration of the nail bed, which weakens the plate-to-bed attachment and raises onycholysis risk.

Psoriasis is the most common systemic condition associated with nail onycholysis. Nail psoriasis is present in up to 50% of people with skin psoriasis and in 80-90% of those who develop psoriatic arthritis.
Both hyper- and hypothyroidism alter nail growth kinetics and can produce onycholysis; treating the thyroid condition typically resolves the nail changes over several months.
Nail plate growth slows with age, increasing the risk of onychomycosis and reducing the nail's natural mechanical resilience, both of which predispose to onycholysis.
People receiving chemotherapy, systemic corticosteroids, or biologic agents are at higher risk for onychomycosis and secondary nail colonisation, both of which cause onycholysis.

riskFactors.factor: Tetracycline or fluoroquinolone use with UV exposure
riskFactors.description: Patients taking these photosensitising antibiotics who are exposed to significant sunlight can develop photo-onycholysis within 2-8 weeks; sun protection and awareness dramatically reduce this risk.
Women develop onycholysis more frequently than men, largely attributable to cosmetic nail practices rather than an intrinsic biological difference.

affectedDemographics: Women aged 20-55 who use artificial or cosmetically treated nails
Adults over 50 with toenail onycholysis secondary to fungal infection
People with psoriasis, in whom nail involvement is common
Patients with thyroid disorders, particularly hyperthyroidism
Healthcare workers and others with prolonged daily wet-work exposure
Patients on photosensitising medications with regular sun exposure
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of onycholysis is clinical, based on the characteristic appearance of nail plate detachment, but establishing the underlying cause requires a systematic evaluation. The clinician inspects the full nail unit noting the pattern of detachment, colour, degree of subungual hyperkeratosis, and involvement of adjacent nails or skin. Fungal infection is the most important cause to exclude because it requires specific antifungal therapy; a nail clipping and subungual debris sample sent for potassium hydroxide preparation, fungal culture, or PCR confirms or rules out onychomycosis. When psoriasis is suspected clinically — especially with pitting, oil-drop discolouration, salmon patches, or joint symptoms — no additional nail test is needed, but a dermatology or rheumatology review is warranted. Thyroid disease should be considered in cases with no obvious mechanical or fungal cause, particularly when both fingernails and toenails are affected: serum TSH is a simple first-line test. A medication history is essential to identify photosensitising drugs. Blood tests are not routinely indicated for isolated traumatic onycholysis but may include a full blood count, iron studies, zinc, and thyroid function when systemic disease is suspected. Dermoscopy of the nail can help distinguish psoriatic oil-drop patterns from fungal or traumatic changes and is increasingly used in specialist practice.

diagnosticTests.test: Potassium hydroxide (KOH) preparation of nail clippings
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects fungal hyphae or spores in the nail to confirm or exclude onychomycosis as the cause.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician cuts a small sample from the separated nail edge and any crumbling subungual debris; a lab processes it with KOH to dissolve keratin and views it under a microscope. Results are available in one to two days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the specific fungal species and allows determination of antifungal susceptibility, guiding treatment choice.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The same nail sample is plated on growth media and incubated; cultures take two to six weeks but confirm viable fungal infection and speciate the organism.

diagnosticTests.test: Nail PCR (periodic acid-Schiff histology or molecular fungal PCR)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides faster and more sensitive detection of fungal DNA than culture, useful when KOH is negative but clinical suspicion remains.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clipped nail sample is sent to a specialist laboratory; results typically return within one week and can detect infection even when the fungus is no longer viable.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for hyperthyroidism or hypothyroidism as a systemic cause of onycholysis when no mechanical, fungal, or psoriatic cause is identified.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A routine blood draw from the arm; result available within hours to one day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Allows a non-invasive magnified view to distinguish the oil-drop pattern of psoriasis, the salmon patch, splinter haemorrhages, and other patterns from traumatic or fungal changes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The dermatologist applies a handheld device with a polarised light lens to the nail; it is painless, takes a few minutes, and requires no preparation.

diagnosticTests.test: Skin swab or nail culture for bacteria
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies Pseudomonas or Staphylococcus species colonising the subungual space when green or purulent discolouration is present.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician inserts a sterile swab beneath the lifted nail edge; the sample is processed in a microbiology laboratory within 24-48 hours.

treatmentOverview:
Management of onycholysis centres on removing the underlying cause, protecting the nail from further trauma and moisture, and treating any secondary infection. Without addressing the cause, no local treatment produces lasting improvement. The first practical step for all subtypes is to trim the separated nail edge back to the line of attachment using clean scissors, eliminating the dead space beneath the lifted plate where bacteria and yeast accumulate.

Fungal onycholysis requires a prolonged course of systemic antifungal therapy: oral terbinafine 250 mg daily for 12 weeks in fingernails and 16-24 weeks in toenails achieves mycological cure in approximately 70-80% of cases and remains the most effective pharmacological option. Topical antifungal lacquers (amorolfine, ciclopirox) are alternatives for superficial or mild cases affecting less than 50% of the nail surface.

Photo-onycholysis resolves after stopping the offending drug and avoiding sun exposure during the regrowth period of six to twelve months. Psoriatic onycholysis may benefit from potent topical corticosteroids or calcipotriol applied under the proximal nail fold for three to six months, intralesional triamcinolone injections into the nail matrix, or, for severe psoriatic arthritis with nail involvement, systemic biologic agents including TNF-alpha and IL-17 inhibitors. Thyroid-related onycholysis responds to achieving euthyroid status, with nail improvement following over three to six months.

Secondary Pseudomonas colonisation producing green-nail syndrome is treated with twice-daily dilute acetic acid soaks. During all phases of regrowth, patients should keep nails trimmed short, wear protective gloves for wet-work activities, and avoid inserting objects beneath the separated nail.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral terbinafine
medicalTreatments.description: The first-line systemic antifungal for onychomycosis-related onycholysis; a once-daily 250 mg tablet taken for 12 weeks in fingernails and 16-24 weeks in toenails. It achieves fungicidal activity by depleting ergosterol in the fungal cell membrane.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mycological cure in approximately 70-80% of toenail onychomycosis cases at 12 months follow-up; clinical cure (clear nail) in 35-50% at one year (NICE TA 258 data).

medicalTreatments.name: Topical antifungals (amorolfine nail lacquer, ciclopirox lacquer)
medicalTreatments.description: Applied to the nail surface once to twice weekly for up to 12 months; suitable for superficial or early fungal onycholysis affecting fewer than 50% of the nail. Much lower systemic absorption than oral agents.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mycological cure in approximately 30-40% of mild distal-subungual onychomycosis at 12 months; significantly less effective than oral terbinafine for established infection.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical corticosteroids or calcipotriol (psoriatic onycholysis)
medicalTreatments.description: Applied to the proximal nail fold and hyponychium in psoriatic onycholysis; high-potency agents (clobetasol propionate) under occlusion can reduce nail bed inflammation and improve attachment. Used for 3-6 months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduction in psoriatic nail score of approximately 40-50% with consistent use over three months in published case series.

medicalTreatments.name: Intralesional corticosteroid injections
medicalTreatments.description: Triamcinolone acetonide injected into the proximal nail fold or nail matrix delivers high local anti-inflammatory concentration for nail psoriasis, minimising systemic exposure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Significant improvement in pitting, onycholysis, and oil-drop changes in approximately 60-70% of patients with psoriatic nail disease within three to six months.

medicalTreatments.name: Dilute acetic acid soaks (Pseudomonas colonisation)
medicalTreatments.description: Twice-daily five-minute soaks in a 1:1 mixture of white vinegar and water create an acid environment hostile to Pseudomonas aeruginosa, clearing the characteristic green pigment and preventing re-colonisation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Green nail syndrome resolves in approximately 4-8 weeks with consistent soaks in the majority of cases; recurrence is prevented by keeping the nail dry.

medicalTreatments.name: Biologic agents (severe psoriatic nail and joint disease)
medicalTreatments.description: TNF-alpha inhibitors (adalimumab, etanercept) and IL-17 inhibitors (secukinumab, ixekizumab) produce significant improvement in nail psoriasis as part of treating systemic psoriatic disease, particularly when psoriatic arthritis is present.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical improvement in nail psoriasis scores of 40-75% at week 24 in randomised controlled trials of biologic therapies for psoriatic arthritis.

alternativeTreatments.name: Tea tree oil
alternativeTreatments.description: Has modest antifungal properties in laboratory studies; limited clinical trial evidence for nail fungus. Sometimes used as a topical adjunct to conventional antifungal therapy by patients preferring a plant-based option.

alternativeTreatments.name: Laser therapy (Nd:YAG or diode laser)
alternativeTreatments.description: Targets fungal organisms through photothermal mechanisms; FDA-cleared for temporary increase of clear nail in onychomycosis but evidence for lasting mycological cure is limited and variable. May be considered when oral antifungals are contraindicated.

alternativeTreatments.name: Protective gloves and nail hygiene measures
alternativeTreatments.description: Consistently wearing waterproof gloves for wet-work activities, keeping nails trimmed short, and avoiding pointed implements beneath the nail are non-pharmacological measures that substantially reduce trauma-related onycholysis recurrence.

whySeeSpecialist: A dermatologist should be consulted when onycholysis persists despite four to six weeks of basic nail care and avoidance of triggers, when the cause is unclear after initial assessment, when psoriatic joint disease is suspected alongside nail changes, or when oral antifungal therapy is being considered in a patient with liver disease or a complex medication history. Specialist assessment also reveals less common causes such as lichen planus, alopecia areata nail involvement, or tumour of the nail bed.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a dermatologist with a specific interest in nail disorders or onychodermatology. Dermoscopy expertise and access to in-office nail culture are useful capabilities. If systemic disease such as psoriasis or thyroid dysfunction is identified, coordinated care with a rheumatologist or endocrinologist respectively improves outcomes. Ask whether the practice can perform nail biopsy if the cause remains uncertain.
preventionStrategies: Keep nails trimmed to just beyond the fingertip so they do not catch or lever against surfaces.
Wear gloves when working with water, detergents, or chemicals for more than a few minutes daily.
Use shoes with adequate toe-box space to prevent repetitive pressure on toenails.
Apply antifungal powder or spray to feet and shoes regularly if prone to athlete's foot, which can progress to nail infection.
Limit use of acrylic or gel nail products and ensure professional removal to avoid mechanical lifting.
Apply sunscreen to the hands and avoid excessive UV exposure when taking photosensitising medications.
Address underlying systemic conditions — thyroid disease, psoriasis — with medical treatment to prevent recurrence.
lifestyleModifications: Trim the separated nail back to the attachment line with clean nail scissors to remove the dead space where organisms proliferate.
Keep the hands and feet as dry as possible; thoroughly dry between toes after bathing.
Avoid inserting any object beneath the nail to clean it, as this worsens separation.
Change socks daily and choose moisture-wicking fabrics if you have toenail onycholysis.
Avoid nail biting and the habit of pulling at loose nail edges, which extends the detachment.
Wear open-toe footwear when possible during warm months to reduce subungual moisture.

recommended: Adequate protein intake to support nail matrix keratin production during regrowth
Foods rich in biotin (eggs, nuts, whole grains), though deficiency is rare and supplementation should be guided by a clinician
Iron-rich foods or supplementation if iron-deficiency anaemia is identified as a contributing factor
Zinc from lean meats, shellfish, and legumes, as zinc deficiency can impair nail adhesion
avoid: No specific dietary restriction is established for onycholysis
Excessive alcohol, which may impair liver tolerance for oral antifungal therapy
Biotin megadoses without clinical indication, as they can interfere with certain laboratory assays

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise does not directly cause or worsen onycholysis but footwear during physical activity matters significantly. Running or gym shoes that are too tight concentrate repetitive pressure on the first and second toenails; replacing worn shoes and wearing correctly sized athletic footwear reduces toenail trauma. After exercise, dry the feet promptly and change into clean socks. Swimming is safe but drying thoroughly between toes afterward limits fungal and Pseudomonas colonisation.
dailyManagement: Trim the detached nail edge every two weeks to keep it at or just beyond the line of attachment.
Apply antifungal lacquer as prescribed to affected nails after bathing, when the nail surface is clean and dry.
Keep a pair of rubber gloves by the kitchen sink as a reminder to wear them for washing up.
Monitor the nail for signs of Pseudomonas: any green tinge warrants review.
Take photographs of affected nails monthly to track progress — full regrowth of a toenail takes 12-18 months, so visible improvement is gradual.
prognosis: The prognosis for onycholysis is good when the underlying cause is identified and corrected. Traumatic onycholysis in patients who stop the damaging behaviour typically regrows a normal nail over six to twelve months. Fungal onycholysis treated with oral terbinafine achieves mycological cure in approximately 70-80% of cases, with a complete clinical cure — a fully clear normal nail — in 35-50% by one year; some degree of permanent nail dystrophy can persist in long-standing cases. Psoriatic onycholysis fluctuates with the activity of skin and joint disease but often responds to targeted anti-psoriatic therapy. Thyroid-related Plummer's nails resolve within three to six months of achieving euthyroid status. The main unfavourable prognostic factor is late diagnosis that allows secondary infection to become established, which extends the course and complicates regrowth.
recoveryTimeline: Fingernail regrowth proceeds at approximately 3 mm per month, so complete replacement of a fully detached fingernail takes about four to six months. Toenails grow more slowly — around 1.5 mm per month — meaning full regrowth of a toenail takes 12-18 months even after successful treatment. During this time the nail may look permanently abnormal. The separated portion does not reattach; the new attachment grows out from the base of the nail. Patients should expect to see visible improvement in fingernails from around three months and in toenails from six months onward.
complications: Secondary Pseudomonas aeruginosa infection producing green-nail syndrome, which prolongs the course and requires specific treatment.
Chronic Candida colonisation of the subungual space, especially in patients with frequent wet-work exposure or immunosuppression.
Permanent nail dystrophy if the nail matrix is damaged by prolonged infection or repeated trauma, leaving a permanently ridged, thickened, or misshapen nail.
Acute paronychia — infection of the nail fold — spreading from subungual bacterial colonisation, causing painful swelling and requiring antibiotic treatment.
Recurrence if the underlying cause is not fully addressed, leading to a cycle of detachment and incomplete regrowth.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Dermatology — Nail Fungus Overview

supportResources.name: NHS — Nail Problems
supportResources.description: Plain-English overview of common nail conditions including onycholysis from the UK National Health Service.

sources.title: Piraccini BM, Alessandrini A. Onychomycosis: a review. J Fungi (Basel). 2015;1(1):30-43

confusedWithConditions.name: Onychogryphosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Onychogryphosis produces dramatic thickening and curved overgrowth of the nail plate without true plate-to-bed separation, whereas onycholysis is characterised by the nail lifting cleanly away from a relatively normal nail bed.

confusedWithConditions.name: Anonychia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Anonychia is complete absence of the nail plate, a structural abnormality, whereas onycholysis involves a nail plate that is present but detached from its bed.

confusedWithConditions.name: Nail psoriasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Psoriatic nail changes include pitting, salmon patches, and subungual hyperkeratosis alongside onycholysis; isolated nail psoriasis without typical skin plaques can be difficult to distinguish from traumatic or fungal onycholysis without biopsy or dermoscopy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acrodermatitis continua of Hallopeau
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acrodermatitis continua causes sterile pustules, periungual redness, and progressive nail loss from pustular psoriasis affecting the fingertips, whereas onycholysis is a painless lifting of the plate without pustules.

relatedConditions.name: Onychogryphosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another common structural nail disorder often seen in the same elderly population, sometimes occurring alongside or confused with onycholysis.

relatedConditions.name: Anonychia
relatedConditions.relevance: The extreme end of nail plate loss, relevant as a differential for patients presenting with minimal or absent nail coverage.

relatedConditions.name: Psoriasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Psoriasis is one of the most common systemic causes of onycholysis; nail involvement occurs in half of all psoriasis patients.

relatedConditions.name: Alopecia areata
relatedConditions.relevance: Alopecia areata can produce nail pitting and trachyonychia alongside hair loss, occasionally coexisting with onycholysis-pattern nail changes.

faqs.question: What is onycholysis?
faqs.answer: Onycholysis is the painless separation of the nail plate from the nail bed, starting at the free edge and spreading toward the base. It produces a whitish or opaque appearance and creates a moist pocket that bacteria and yeast can colonise.

faqs.question: What causes onycholysis?
faqs.answer: Common causes include trauma from acrylic nails or tight footwear, fungal infection, psoriasis, thyroid disease, and medications such as tetracyclines that cause photo-onycholysis. Identifying the specific cause is essential because each requires a different treatment.

faqs.question: Is onycholysis contagious?
faqs.answer: Onycholysis itself is not contagious. However, if the underlying cause is a fungal infection, the fungus can spread to other nails or to close contacts through shared footwear or floors. Treating the infection and maintaining nail hygiene reduces this risk.

faqs.question: Will the nail grow back after onycholysis?
faqs.answer: Yes. The separated portion does not reattach, but the nail grows out from the base over several months. Fingernails take approximately 4-6 months and toenails 12-18 months to fully regrow. Success depends on removing the underlying cause.

faqs.question: Should I cut the detached part of the nail?
faqs.answer: Yes. Trimming the separated nail back to the point where it is still attached removes the dead space where bacteria and fungi accumulate. Use clean scissors and cut back only to the line of attachment. Do not force the nail away from the bed further.

faqs.question: How is onycholysis treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on the cause. Fungal cases require oral terbinafine for 12-24 weeks. Psoriatic onycholysis responds to topical or injected corticosteroids or systemic psoriasis therapy. Traumatic cases resolve after removing the injury source. Photo-onycholysis clears after stopping the offending drug.

faqs.question: How long does onycholysis take to heal?
faqs.answer: Fingernails take 4-6 months to regrow fully; toenails take 12-18 months. Improvement becomes visible after 2-3 months for fingernails and 6 months for toenails. The pace depends on how completely the underlying cause has been addressed.

faqs.question: Why has my nail turned green?
faqs.answer: A green nail is a sign that Pseudomonas aeruginosa bacteria have colonised the moist space beneath the separated nail. This is called green-nail syndrome and is treated with twice-daily dilute vinegar soaks and, in some cases, antibiotic ear or eye drops applied under the nail.

faqs.question: Can thyroid disease cause onycholysis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hyperthyroidism can cause Plummer's nails — soft, quickly growing nails that separate from the distal end, classically on the ring finger. Hypothyroidism is a less common but recognised cause. Treating the thyroid abnormality resolves the nail changes within a few months.

faqs.question: Can onycholysis be a sign of psoriasis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Nail onycholysis is present in up to 50% of people with psoriasis and in 80-90% of those with psoriatic arthritis. An 'oil-drop' or salmon patch beneath the nail alongside lifting strongly suggests nail psoriasis and warrants dermatology review.

faqs.question: What medicines cause onycholysis?
faqs.answer: Photo-onycholysis is most commonly caused by tetracyclines (especially doxycycline), psoralens, chlorpromazine, and fluoroquinolones. The separation occurs after sun exposure during or after taking these drugs. Stopping the medication and protecting hands from sunlight allows the nail to regrow normally.

faqs.question: Is onycholysis painful?
faqs.answer: The separation itself is usually painless. Tenderness can develop if the lifted nail catches on clothing, or if secondary infection causes inflammation of the surrounding skin. Pain, redness, and swelling around the nail fold suggest a complicating paronychia requiring medical attention.

faqs.question: How do I prevent onycholysis from coming back?
faqs.answer: Keep nails trimmed short, wear gloves for wet work and cleaning, choose well-fitted footwear, and avoid acrylic nails if they have caused problems before. Treat any fungal infection fully and manage underlying conditions such as psoriasis or thyroid disease.

faqs.question: Can I get a manicure if I have onycholysis?
faqs.answer: It is best to avoid acrylic, gel, or sculptured nails until the nail has fully regrown, as these products can worsen the separation. Gentle, water-based nail polish is generally safe but should be removed carefully. Inform the nail technician and avoid cutting into the lifted edge.

faqs.question: Does onycholysis affect toenails differently from fingernails?
faqs.answer: Toenail onycholysis is more often caused by fungal infection or footwear pressure, while fingernail onycholysis more commonly results from trauma, cosmetic nail products, or systemic causes like thyroid disease. Toenails also grow much more slowly, so recovery takes significantly longer.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor for onycholysis?
faqs.answer: See a doctor if the separation does not improve after 4-6 weeks of basic nail care, if the nail is green or painful, if multiple nails are affected, if you have skin symptoms of psoriasis, or if the problem keeps recurring. A dermatologist can identify the cause and direct specific treatment.

faqs.question: Can onycholysis be a sign of cancer?
faqs.answer: Rarely, an underlying subungual melanoma or squamous cell carcinoma of the nail bed can cause nail plate separation. These are uncommon but should be considered when onycholysis affects a single digit, is painless and persistent, and does not have a clear cause. A nail biopsy can clarify the diagnosis.

faqs.question: Is biotin helpful for onycholysis?
faqs.answer: Biotin supplementation is sometimes used for brittle nails, but evidence specifically for onycholysis is limited. It may be of benefit when deficiency has been confirmed. It does not treat the underlying cause of nail separation and should not replace cause-directed treatment.

faqs.question: What is the difference between onycholysis and onychomycosis?
faqs.answer: Onychomycosis is a fungal infection of the nail that is one cause of onycholysis. Onycholysis simply describes nail plate separation from any cause. A nail with fungal infection often shows onycholysis alongside thickening and crumbling, but onycholysis can exist without any fungal involvement.

faqs.question: How does a dermatologist diagnose the cause of onycholysis?
faqs.answer: The dermatologist examines the nail pattern, performs dermoscopy, takes nail clippings for KOH preparation and fungal culture, and orders blood tests such as TSH if a systemic cause is suspected. A nail biopsy is reserved for unclear cases or when a nail bed tumour is a concern.

metaDescription: Onycholysis is painless nail plate separation from the bed, caused by trauma, fungal infection, psoriasis or thyroid disease. Learn treatments and recovery.
